steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.13
Test Case Name: Multiple welcome messages triggered on app install

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
To verify that the bot triggers exactly one comprehensive message upon installation. If the bot triggers multiple separate welcome messages on install (e.g., greeting in one message, commands in another), the test fails. The bot must not send multiple separate messages (e.g., greeting in one message, commands in another). For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, a repeated configuration-style message containing way‑forward guidance is considered a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Preparation: Ensure the app is fully uninstalled from the test environment.
Installation: Install the app.
Observation: Count how many distinct bot messages appear immediately after the "added" system notification.
Content Check: If multiple messages appear, assess whether they should have been merged into a single Adaptive Card.
Identify Failure:
Pass: One cohesive message or a single Adaptive Card.
Fail: Two or more messages sent in quick succession.
Non‑Integrated Exception: A repeated configuration-style message with way‑forward guidance is acceptable.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. The message count must reflect only proactive bot messages.
After installation, WAIT until the page is fully settled AND give the conversation a few extra seconds so that ALL bot messages (including any delayed second/third message) have arrived and rendered before capturing. Do not capture while messages are still streaming in — a premature frame can under-count the messages.
Capture ONE settled screenshot showing the "added" system notification together with the bot message(s) that follow it. The screenshot(s) must make the TOTAL message count clear.
If the messages do not all fit in one viewport, scroll through the conversation one segment at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so that every distinct bot message after the notification is captured. Every scroll position must be its own distinct, settled screenshot.
Capture these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the message count is fully evidenced.
On FAIL (two or more messages), ensure the settled screenshots clearly show all the separate messages in succession as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The bot must trigger only one welcome message/card upon installation.
All introductory content (Value Prop, Commands, Links) must appear in that single message.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, a configuration-style message with guidance is treated as PASS.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.13
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The bot triggered three separate messages: a greeting, then an image, then a command list. These must be consolidated."
Impact: Multiple messages create a spam‑like first impression for users.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot showing multiple bot messages.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.2
Test Case Name: In personal scope, bot does not send a pro-active "Welcome" message

Objective:
To verify that the bot automatically triggers a single, comprehensive "Welcome" message upon installation in the personal scope. If the bot does not send any proactive welcome message in the personal scope (chat stays silent until the user messages first), the test fails. The message must educate the user on the bot's value, provide onboarding steps, and list commands in an interactive format. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be considered a PASS.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Install the App: Add the bot to the personal (1:1) scope.
Monitor Proactive Trigger: Confirm the bot sends a proactive message immediately without user input.
Check Frequency: Ensure the message triggers only once per installation.
Content Audit (Value Prop): Verify the welcome message clearly explains what the bot does and why it is useful.
Content Audit (Integration/Config): Ensure step‑by‑step setup or integration guidance is present.
Command Review: Confirm all supported bot commands are listed and briefly described.
Interaction Check: Verify the presence of an Adaptive Card with buttons such as "Sign‑up" or "Contact Us."

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. The welcome message must be received proactively.
After installation, switch to the 1:1 chat and WAIT until the page is fully settled and the proactive welcome message / Adaptive Card is completely rendered (allow late-loading card content and streamed text to finish before capturing).
Capture ONE settled screenshot showing the welcome message immediately after the system "added to chat" notification. This is the primary evidence frame.
If the Adaptive Card is taller than the visible viewport, scroll through it one section at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so that the Value Proposition, Configuration Steps, Command List, and the Sign‑up/Contact buttons are each captured in their own distinct, settled frame. Every scroll position must be its own screenshot — do not rely on a single frame to cover the whole card.
Capture all of these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until every required evidence screenshot (welcome frame + all scrolled sections) has been captured.
Failure evidence: If the bot stays silent (no proactive message), wait for the page to settle, then capture ONE screenshot of the silent chat showing only the "added to chat" notification.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final on-screen state, not a transitional/loading frame.

Expected Result:
The bot sends exactly one proactive welcome message.
The message uses an Adaptive Card.
The message contains: Value Proposition, Configuration Steps, Command List, and a Sign‑up/Contact link.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, this scenario should PASS once the run initiates correctly.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.2
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The bot did not trigger a proactive message; it responded only after the user sent 'Hello.'"
Example: "The message lacked a command list."
Missing Elements: List missing components (e.g., missing Sign‑up link or configuration steps).
Evidence: (Screenshot(s) showing the bot's proactive message immediately after the system 'added to chat' notification, plus scrolled views of the full Adaptive Card.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.14
Test Case Name: Welcome message does not state the correct app name

Objective of this Test Case:
To ensure that the name used within the welcome message text (and the Adaptive Card header) matches the official App Name in the manifest and marketplace listing. If the welcome message states an incorrect, outdated, placeholder, or mismatched app name (vs. the manifest/marketplace listing), the test fails. This includes checking for typos, outdated names, or placeholder names. For Non‑Integrated accounts, the tool must sideload the package and search for the app under "Built for your org" and click Add/Open to start validation.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Check Manifest/Listing: Identify the official App Name (e.g., "ProjectFlow Pro").
Install the App: Trigger the welcome message in personal or channel scope. For Non‑Integrated accounts, sideload the package and access it via Built for your org → Add/Open.
Cross‑Reference Names: Read the welcome message text and check for:
Mismatches (e.g., app says "ProjectFlow" but welcome message says "TaskMaster").
Placeholders (e.g., "Welcome to [App Name]").
Branding inconsistencies or unapproved abbreviations.
Case Sensitivity/Typos: Confirm capitalization and spelling exactly match the official name.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. Read and compare the displayed names only.
WAIT until the page is fully settled and the welcome message / Adaptive Card is completely rendered before capturing (allow late-loading card content to finish).
Capture ONE settled screenshot of the Teams sidebar / app header showing the OFFICIAL App Name, and ONE settled screenshot of the welcome message text (and Adaptive Card header) showing the name as used by the bot. If both the sidebar name and the welcome message are visible together, a single combined settled screenshot is also acceptable in addition.
The two screenshots together form the required side‑by‑side evidence (official name vs. welcome-message name).
Capture these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until both name screenshots are captured.
On FAIL (mismatch, placeholder, or typo), ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the conflicting names as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must clearly state the correct, official app name.
The name must match the marketplace/manifest listing consistently throughout the message.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.14
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "App is listed as 'Invoicer Plus' but welcome message says 'Welcome to InvoiceHelper'. Developer must update branding."
Impact: Incorrect naming creates user confusion and may cause security concerns.
Evidence: (Side‑by‑side screenshot of the App Name in Teams Sidebar vs. Welcome Message.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.12
Test Case Name: Welcome message has content issues (irrelevant, repetitive, unrelated to bot functionality in the scope, inappropriate or unsuitable for workplace)

Objective:
To verify that the welcome message content is high‑quality, relevant to the installation scope (Personal, Channel, or Group Chat), and professional. If the welcome message content is irrelevant, repetitive, unrelated to the bot's functionality in the installed scope, or inappropriate/unsuitable for a workplace, the test fails. The message must provide clear value and instructions without repetition or irrelevant content. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be considered a PASS.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Scope Verification: Install the bot in the relevant scope (e.g., Team Channel).
Content Audit: Review the text for irrelevance, repetition, and inappropriate tone.
Check "Who‑Installed" Context: In Channel scope, verify acknowledgment of the user who added the bot.
Feature Alignment: Confirm the message matches the app's value proposition from the AppSource description.
Instructional Clarity: Ensure setup instructions and commands are clearly stated and easy to follow.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. Review the rendered message content only.
After installing in the relevant scope, WAIT until the page is fully settled and the welcome message / Adaptive Card is completely rendered before capturing (allow late-loading card content and streamed text to finish).
Capture ONE settled screenshot of the welcome message. If the message is taller than the visible viewport, scroll through it one section at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so the full content (value proposition, who‑installed acknowledgment where applicable, setup steps, and command list) is captured. Every scroll position must be its own distinct, settled screenshot.
In Channel scope, ensure at least one settled screenshot clearly shows the "who‑installed" acknowledgment.
Capture these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until all required evidence screenshots are taken.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the issue (e.g., placeholder text, repetition, unsupported feature references, unprofessional tone) as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The message is professional, concise, and appropriate for the scope.
It clearly states the value proposition, who installed the app (where applicable), setup steps, and available commands.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the test should PASS once the tool starts correctly.
Expected Result:
The message is professional, concise, and appropriate for the scope.
It clearly states the value proposition, who installed the app (where applicable), setup steps, and available commands.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the test should PASS once the tool starts correctly.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.12
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The message contains placeholder text and references unsupported features."
Example: "Tone is unprofessional (e.g., slang or excessive emojis)."
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of the welcome message with highlighted issues.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.16
Test Case Name: Welcome message redirecting user to another Teams app
Objective of this Test Case:
To ensure the welcome message acts as a functional "Quick Start" guide for the current app. If the welcome message redirects or promotes the user toward another Teams app (download/switch links, buttons, or deep links to other app IDs) instead of focusing on the current app, the test fails. It must encourage the user's first in-app action and clearly list available commands, rather than promoting other Teams apps. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the same configuration-style onboarding message contains clear way‑forward guidance and a contact link, this is considered a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger Welcome Message: Install the app and receive the proactive greeting.
Scan for App Redirection: Look for links/buttons encouraging users to download or switch to other Teams apps, or deep links to other app IDs.
Evaluate Task Nudging: Check whether the message encourages an immediate first action (e.g., "Create your first task…").
Audit Command List: Ensure primary commands are briefly described in the welcome message.
Assess Interaction: Confirm the user can begin using the bot immediately based on the provided instructions. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated configuration-style message with way‑forward guidance and a contact link is acceptable.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. Review the rendered message, buttons, and links only.
After installation, WAIT until the page is fully settled and the welcome message / Adaptive Card is completely rendered before capturing (allow late-loading card content and streamed text to finish).
Capture ONE settled screenshot of the welcome message that clearly shows its buttons/links and command list. If the card is taller than the visible viewport, scroll through it one section at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so the first-action call-to-action, command overview, and ALL buttons/links (including any that redirect to other apps) are captured. Every scroll position must be its own distinct, settled screenshot.
Capture these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until all required evidence screenshots are taken.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the issue (e.g., a button/link promoting a different Teams app, or a missing command list) as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must not redirect users to other Teams apps.
It must include a first-action call to action and a concise command overview.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the message is valid if it includes way‑forward guidance and a contact link.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.16
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "Welcome message includes a button promoting a different Teams app and lacks command guidance."
Required Correction: Remove external app redirects and include a 'Getting Started' instruction section.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot showing the prohibited redirect or missing commands.)

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.15
Test Case Name: Welcome message acting as a marketing vehicle by displaying competitor chat based collaborative platform names

Objective of this Test Case:
To ensure that the welcome message remains focused on the Microsoft ecosystem. Mentions of competitors used as marketing or promotional text must be considered non‑compliant.

Install the App: Trigger the proactive welcome message in personal or channel scope.
Scan for Competitor Keywords: Check for mentions such as Slack, Google Workspace/Google Chat, Zoom, Webex, Discord.

Pass (Interoperability): e.g., "Use this bot to import your Slack archives into Teams."
Fail (Marketing/Distraction): e.g., "Check out our Slack app too!" or "Better than Google Chat."

After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so the ENTIRE message text and all buttons/links are captured (competitor mentions can appear anywhere, including the footer or buttons). Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.

Competitor names must not appear unless necessary to describe functional interoperability.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.17
Test Case Name: Welcome message has links to other app marketplaces
Objective of this Test Case:
To verify that the proactive welcome message is free of any hyperlinks, buttons, or QR codes that direct the user to any app marketplace. If the welcome message contains links to other app marketplaces (e.g., AppSource, Google Play, Apple App Store, Azure Marketplace), the test fails. This prevents circular navigation back to AppSource and platform diversion to non‑Microsoft app stores.
Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger Welcome Message: Install the app in the personal or channel scope to receive the onboarding message.
Inspect All Links & Buttons: Check every interactive element in the message.
Search for Prohibited Domains: Identify URLs pointing to:
appsource.microsoft.com
play.google.com
apps.apple.com
azuremarketplace.microsoft.com

Check for "Download App" Prompts: Look for text instructing the user to install a mobile app through store links.
QR Code Scan (if applicable): Ensure the QR code does not resolve to any marketplace listing.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):

This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. Inspect the rendered links, buttons, and QR codes only.
After installation, WAIT until the page is fully settled and the welcome message / Adaptive Card is completely rendered before capturing (allow late-loading card content, buttons, and any QR image to finish loading).
Capture ONE settled screenshot of the welcome message showing its full text and interactive elements. If the message is taller than the visible viewport, scroll through it one section at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so that EVERY link, button, "Download App" prompt, and QR code is captured (prohibited marketplace links can appear anywhere, including footers or buttons). Every scroll position must be its own distinct, settled screenshot.
Capture these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until all interactive elements are evidenced.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the prohibited marketplace link(s), store button(s), or QR code as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must not contain links to AppSource or any other app store.
All links must lead to functional destinations such as a support page, privacy policy, or account sign‑up page.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.17
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The welcome message includes a 'Download on the App Store' button and an AppSource link. These must be removed."
Correction Suggestion: Remove marketplace buttons. If users need a mobile companion app, redirect them to a developer landing page instead.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of the welcome message showing prohibited marketplace links.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.8
Test Case Name: App limitations not called out in bot welcome message

Objective:
To verify that the bot's welcome message is a high‑utility Adaptive Card containing the value proposition, configuration steps, command list, support links, and any required disclosures (such as limitations or notification‑only status). Bot responses may contain hyperlinks for contact or help documentation; if hovering over them changes the cursor to a hand icon, this indicates external navigation and is a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Trigger Message: Install the app to receive the proactive welcome message.
Evaluate Format: Confirm the message uses an Adaptive Card with interactive buttons, not plain text.
Content Audit – Essential Elements:
Value Proposition: Check if it explains what the app does.
Sign‑up/Contact Link: Confirm a clear way‑forward action exists.
Help Documentation: Verify the presence of a user guide or FAQ link.
Commands: Ensure supported commands are listed with brief descriptions.
Content Audit – Disclosures:
Limitations: Confirm disclosure if an active subscription/account is required.
Bot Type: If notification‑only, confirm it clearly states non‑responsiveness.
Check for "Dead Ends": Ensure the user knows exactly what to do next.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. Review the rendered card, links, and hover state only.
After installation, WAIT until the page is fully settled and the welcome message / Adaptive Card is completely rendered before capturing (allow late-loading card content and streamed text to finish).
Capture ONE settled screenshot of the welcome message. If the card is taller than the visible viewport, scroll through it one section at a time. After EACH scroll, wait for the view to settle, then capture a SEPARATE screenshot so ALL required elements are captured: Value Proposition, Configuration Steps, Command List, Sign‑up/Contact link, Help/FAQ link, and any Limitations / notification‑only disclosure. Every scroll position must be its own distinct, settled screenshot.
For the hyperlink hover check, hover over a contact/help hyperlink, wait for the view to settle, then capture a separate screenshot showing the cursor as a hand icon over the link (evidence of external navigation), where this is observable.
Capture these as the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until all required elements (and the hover state, if applicable) are evidenced.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshots clearly show the missing or incorrect section (e.g., absent disclosure, missing Support link) as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.

Expected Result:
The welcome message must be an Adaptive Card containing all required elements.
All links must be functional.
Required limitations or usage details must be clearly stated.
Hyperlinks showing a hand cursor on hover are compliant.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.8
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The message is missing the required disclosure about subscription requirements and does not include a Support link."
Missing Components: List missing items (e.g., Value Prop, Command List, Notification‑only disclaimer).
Evidence: (Attach screenshot of Welcome Message showing missing/incorrect sections.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.9
Test Case Name: Welcome message not triggering on app install in personal scope, instead triggering when user sends Help command

Objective of this Test Case:
To verify that the bot initiates communication proactively and automatically when added to the personal scope. The welcome message must appear on installation, without any user input — and must NOT depend on the user sending a "Help" command to surface it. If the welcome message only appears after "Help" is typed, the bot is reactive (not proactive) and the test fails. This ensures users are immediately aware of the bot's presence and capabilities. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be considered a PASS.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Environment Setup: Ensure the app is uninstalled from the user's personal scope.
Installation: Navigate to the app store and install the app for Personal use.
Observation: Switch immediately to the bot's 1:1 chat interface.
Verification of Trigger:
Pass Criteria: Bot sends a welcome message right after the system notification "User has added [App Name] to the chat."
Fail Criteria: Chat remains silent until the user sends a message.
Failure Analysis: If the message appears only after the user types "Help," classify it as reactive instead of proactive.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
This is an observation-only capture — do NOT type anything into the chat. The welcome message must be received passively.
After switching to the 1:1 chat, WAIT until the page is fully settled and BOTH the system notification "User has added [App Name] to the chat" AND the proactive welcome message are completely rendered and visible together in the same view (allow late-arriving/streamed bot text to finish rendering before capturing).
THEN capture exactly ONE settled screenshot that shows the welcome message immediately following the "added to chat" notification. This single frame is the required PASS evidence.
Capture this screenshot as the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the settled evidence screenshot has been captured.
Failure evidence: If the chat stays silent (Fail Criteria), still wait for the page to settle, then capture ONE screenshot of the silent chat showing only the "added to chat" notification with no bot reply.
Ensure the captured frame reflects the final on-screen state (notification + welcome message, or notification + silence) — not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The welcome message must trigger automatically upon installation.
The user should not need to prompt the bot to receive onboarding information.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool starts correctly, this test is PASS.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.9
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The bot failed to send a proactive message. It only responded after 'Help' was typed, making it reactive instead of proactive."
Evidence: (Screenshot showing the proactive welcome message immediately after the "added to chat" notification.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.10
Test Case Name: Welcome message triggers every time user send Help command to the bot

Objective:
To verify that the bot does not use the full "Welcome" message as its response to the "Help" command. If the bot triggers the full Welcome message every time the user sends the Help command, the test fails. The Welcome message is a one‑time onboarding event; the Help command must return a focused response with guidance, commands, or support links. For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly starts the run, this scenario should be treated as a PASS.

Detailed Execution Steps:
Initial Installation: Install the bot and observe the proactive Welcome message (if shown).
Invoke Help: Type Help or select it from the menu in 1:1 chat.
Compare Responses: Compare the Help response with the original Welcome message.
Evaluate Focus: Check whether the Help response provides targeted guidance or simply repeats the Welcome card.
Identify Redundancy: If the Help response is identical or nearly identical to the Welcome message, the test fails—unless running under a Non‑Integrated account scenario.

Screenshot Capture (evidence — TWO screenshots required):
WELCOME screenshot (observation — do NOT type anything yet): After installation, WAIT until the page is fully settled and the proactive Welcome message is completely rendered, THEN capture ONE settled screenshot of the Welcome message.
HELP screenshot (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["Help"]. This types "Help", submits it, waits for the bot's Help RESPONSE card to fully render and settle, and captures the response automatically — so the evidence frame shows the Help reply, NOT the moment "Help" was typed. (Do not screenshot before the bot replies.)
If either message is taller than the viewport, scroll through it one section at a time and, after each scroll, wait for the view to settle and capture an ADDITIONAL screenshot so the full content of each is captured.
The Help-response capture must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until BOTH the Welcome screenshot and the rendered, settled Help-response screenshot have been captured.
On FAIL (Help repeats the Welcome card), ensure the two screenshots clearly show the duplicated content for side‑by‑side comparison.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The Welcome message appears only once, during installation.
The Help command must return a distinct, focused response.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, once the tool correctly initiates the run, this scenario is considered PASS.
Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.10
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The bot returned the same Welcome card for every Help command. Suggested correction: Provide a shorter, task‑oriented Help message."
User Impact: Users seeking assistance must re‑read onboarding content, reducing efficiency and cluttering the chat history.
Evidence: (Attach screenshot comparing the Welcome message and Help response.)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.11
Test Case Name: Welcome message triggers for any bot command

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
To verify that the bot's onboarding logic is correctly isolated. If the welcome message/Adaptive Card triggers for any bot command (reappearing as a prefix to, or instead of, a functional command's output), the test fails. The welcome message/Adaptive Card must appear only once during installation and must not reappear as a prefix or response to functional commands. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot returns the same configuration-style message that includes a clear way‑forward guidance message, this is considered a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Initial Installation: Install the bot in the personal scope and confirm the initial proactive welcome message.
Command Execution: Send a functional command (e.g., status, settings, task).
Observation:
If the bot repeats the welcome message along with the requested data → Fail.
If the bot triggers the welcome message instead of executing the command → Fail.
Repetitive Testing: Send 2–3 different commands to ensure the welcome card does not appear again.
Evaluate for "Spam": Confirm the bot does not repeatedly force onboarding material on the user. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated message containing way‑forward guidance is acceptable.

Screenshot Capture (evidence — welcome message + one screenshot per command):

Welcome (observation): After installation, WAIT until the page is settled and the proactive welcome message is fully rendered, THEN capture ONE settled screenshot of it.
Functional commands (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["status", "settings", "task"] (use the app's actual functional commands if different). This submits each command one at a time, waits for each RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures a SEPARATE settled screenshot of each response — so every command has its own evidence frame showing the functional output (not the moment the command was typed).
If any response is taller than the viewport, scroll through it one section at a time and, after each scroll, wait for the view to settle and capture an ADDITIONAL screenshot so the full content is captured.
The last command's response screenshot must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the welcome screenshot and every command-response screenshot have been captured.
On FAIL, ensure the screenshot clearly shows the welcome message reappearing as a prefix to — or instead of — the command's functional output as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.

Expected Result:
The welcome message must appear only once after installation.
Subsequent commands must respond with only the requested functional output.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, repeated configuration-style responses with way‑forward guidance are considered PASS.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.11
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The bot triggers the full Welcome Message every time a command is sent. Suggested correction: Implement proper state handling between onboarding and message events."
Technical Note: The bot may be missing a state‑check separating membersAdded from message activities.
Evidence: Attach a screenshot.
steps.details:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.11
Test Case Name: Welcome message triggers for any bot command

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
To verify that the bot's onboarding logic is correctly isolated. If the welcome message/Adaptive Card triggers for any bot command (reappearing as a prefix to, or instead of, a functional command's output), the test fails. The welcome message/Adaptive Card must appear only once during installation and must not reappear as a prefix or response to functional commands. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot returns the same configuration-style message that includes a clear way‑forward guidance message, this is considered a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Initial Installation: Install the bot in the personal scope and confirm the initial proactive welcome message.
Command Execution: Send a functional command (e.g., status, settings, task).
Observation:
If the bot repeats the welcome message along with the requested data → Fail.
If the bot triggers the welcome message instead of executing the command → Fail.
Repetitive Testing: Send 2–3 different commands to ensure the welcome card does not appear again.
Evaluate for "Spam": Confirm the bot does not repeatedly force onboarding material on the user. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a repeated message containing way‑forward guidance is acceptable.

Screenshot Capture (evidence — welcome message + one screenshot per command):

Welcome (observation): After installation, WAIT until the page is settled and the proactive welcome message is fully rendered, THEN capture ONE settled screenshot of it.
Functional commands (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field, then call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs: ["status", "settings", "task"] (use the app's actual functional commands if different). This submits each command one at a time, waits for each RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures a SEPARATE settled screenshot of each response — so every command has its own evidence frame showing the functional output (not the moment the command was typed).
If any response is taller than the viewport, scroll through it one section at a time and, after each scroll, wait for the view to settle and capture an ADDITIONAL screenshot so the full content is captured.
The last command's response screenshot must be the LAST action before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the welcome screenshot and every command-response screenshot have been captured.
On FAIL, ensure the screenshot clearly shows the welcome message reappearing as a prefix to — or instead of — the command's functional output as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.

Expected Result:
The welcome message must appear only once after installation.
Subsequent commands must respond with only the requested functional output.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, repeated configuration-style responses with way‑forward guidance are considered PASS.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.4.11
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons:
Example: "The bot triggers the full Welcome Message every time a command is sent. Suggested correction: Implement proper state handling between onboarding and message events."
Technical Note: The bot may be missing a state‑check separating membersAdded from message activities.
Evidence: Attach a screenshot.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.5
Test Case Name: Bot response contains official Microsoft product images or avatars. Please use your own assets in your app. Use of Microsoft product images in your app is not allowed. You may only copy, modify, distribute, display, license, or sell Microsoft copyrighted product images if you are granted explicit permission within the End-User License Agreement (EULA) or license terms that accompany the content or are provided in the following guidelines.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot does not use official Microsoft copyrighted assets—including product logos (Teams, Outlook, Word, etc.), official product icons, or Microsoft‑branded avatars—within its interface unless explicitly permitted. Bot responses may include hyperlinks (e.g., help docs or contact links). If hovering over such hyperlinks shows a hand cursor, this simply indicates external navigation and is a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Inspect Bot Profile: Check the bot's avatar/icon in chat list and header for any Microsoft product logo or derivative.
Review Welcome Message: Ensure the welcome card or text does not contain Microsoft product imagery.
Audit Cards and Task Modules: Trigger multiple bot responses (Hero Cards, Adaptive Cards, Task Modules) and examine all images, icons, or backgrounds.
Identify Infringements: Look for Microsoft product icons used as the bot's own branding or button icons.
Check Documentation/Help: Ensure help cards do not use Microsoft marketing images. Hyperlinks present in the help content are acceptable if they display a hand cursor on hover.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence (its catalog imagery is not the bot's own branding).
BOT PROFILE (observation): Capture ONE settled screenshot of the bot's avatar/icon as shown in the chat header (and chat list, where visible), so any Microsoft product logo or derivative used as the bot's own branding is evidenced.
WELCOME message (observation FIRST, before typing anything): Anchor the view by scrolling the conversation to the TOP so the "added to chat" installation notification is visible; the Welcome message is the bot message immediately following it. WAIT until the page is fully settled and the Welcome card is completely rendered, then capture ONE settled screenshot showing the Welcome card's full imagery/icons. If it is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position. Capture ONLY the Welcome card — not the empty-state landing screen, the store page, or later command output.
CARDS / TASK MODULES / HELP (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): For each response under audit (e.g., a card-producing command, then ["help"]), locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single command. Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled card.
If a Task Module (modal) opens, wait for it to fully render and settle, then capture ONE screenshot of the modal showing all its images/icons/backgrounds.
For each captured card/modal/help response, if it is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so every image, icon, button icon, and background is evidenced.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own avatar, Welcome card, response card, Task Module, or help content. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content from other test steps.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL until the bot avatar, the Welcome card, and the triggered cards/Task Modules/help content have all been captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the unauthorized Microsoft product image, logo, icon, or avatar (e.g., an Outlook/Teams/Word icon used as the bot's own branding or a button icon) as failure evidence.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:
The app must use its own unique branding and not use Microsoft‑owned product images or avatars in a way that implies it is an official Microsoft product. Hyperlinks with hand‑cursor hover are compliant.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot uses the Outlook icon for an Email feature. Suggested correction: Replace it with a neutral mail icon."
Example: "The bot avatar matches the Microsoft 365 logo. Suggested correction: Upload a unique branded logo."
Evidence: [Insert Screenshot showing the unauthorized Microsoft asset]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.1
Test Case Name: Bot response card action buttons redirect outside Teams without warning. Action buttons in bot response cards that redirect outside Teams must make the redirection clear for the user by using text such as "View in…" or "This way to…"
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that any action button inside a bot card that redirects to an external website/browser clearly indicates the redirection in the button text (e.g., "View in…", "Open in…", "This way to…").
Updated Logic:
If the bot shares a bare hyperlink only (e.g., https://example.com) with no descriptive text/context, mark WARNING (not PASS/FAIL).
If no external indicator exists on links but a hyperlink is present and hovering changes the cursor to a hand icon, mark WARNING with:
"There is a hyperlink added in bot response. Please verify the link."
Additional Scope Rule: If the warning condition is observed in only one instance (e.g., a Task Module button lacks external signage) and other potential instances (e.g., hyperlinks in Help response) are not found, the warning message must reflect scope:
"Warning captured for only one instance. Other instances (e.g., links in Help response) were not found."
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (ENFORCED ORDER)
Welcome Message Content:
Trigger or reset to show the Welcome message. Inspect all text, buttons, and hyperlinks in the Welcome message. For every hyperlink/text link: hover and note if the cursor becomes a hand icon. Check if external indicators exist in the label (e.g., "Open in Browser", "View in Web Portal", ↗️). Record any bare links or links without external indicators.
View Prompts (Compose Tray) & Run All Commands
Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray. type all listed commands under view prompts and capture each response (text/cards/errors). Skip running Help command if it listed under view prompts. For each response, inspect for hyperlinks, do the hover check, and verify label indicators.
Help Command & Actionables
type help → confirm in‑app reply (message/card). Extract actionables/supported commands from the Help response: Buttons/CTAs, explicitly listed commands, deep‑link actions. Note any hyperlinks for later hover check. type all of the Help‑listed commands (one by one) → trigger each button/command exactly as shown and capture the response. Check links in each response: Hover → hand cursor? Label shows external indicator (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️)?
Generic Commands
Send "hi" and "hello". Analyze responses for hyperlinks (hover test) and presence/absence of external indicators.
One Invalid Command
Send one invalid input (e.g., "xyz123"). Analyze response for hyperlinks (hover test) and indicators.
Across all steps (1–5):
If a bare hyperlink is found → WARNING. If a hyperlink shows hand cursor on hover but no external indicator in label → WARNING with the specified message. If such cases appear in any of one step, use the scoped warning text indicating other instances were not found. Collect and list all steps where hyperlinks with hand‑cursor behavior were detected.
Screenshot Capture (evidence):

FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before typing anything. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
WELCOME message (Step 1 — observation FIRST, before typing anything): Anchor the view by scrolling the conversation to the TOP so the "added to chat" installation notification is visible; the Welcome message is the bot message immediately following it. WAIT until the page is fully settled and the Welcome card is completely rendered, then capture ONE settled screenshot showing all of its text, action buttons, and hyperlinks (with their exact labels). If the Welcome card is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position. For each hyperlink/button, hover the pointer over it and capture ONE settled screenshot showing the cursor state (hand/pointer = external redirection) so the label-vs-indicator check is evidenced.
VIEW PROMPTS list (Step 2 — observation): Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray, wait for it to fully render and settle, and capture ONE settled screenshot of the full command list. Scroll-per-section with a SEPARATE settled screenshot if it is taller than the viewport.
COMMANDS / HELP / GENERIC / INVALID (Steps 2–5 — interaction, use the deterministic capture tool): For each input under test — every View Prompts command (skip Help here if it is listed there), then ["help"], then each Help‑listed command one by one, then ["hi"], ["hello"], and one invalid input ["xyz123"] — locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = that single command. Each call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled response card/text. The evidence frame must show the settled response, NOT the moment of typing.
For every response that contains a hyperlink or action button, hover the pointer over it and capture ONE settled screenshot showing the hand/pointer cursor, plus the button/link label, so the "external indicator present?" determination is evidenced. If a card/Task Module is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own Welcome card, command list, response cards, or hover state. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL/WARNING until the Welcome message, the View Prompts list and command responses, the help response and its command responses, the generic responses, and the invalid response have all been captured and settled, with hover frames for every hyperlink/button found.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the action button that redirects externally WITHOUT descriptive labeling. On WARNING, ensure the screenshot shows the bare/raw hyperlink (no context) or the hyperlink whose hover shows a hand cursor but whose label has no external indicator.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result
Buttons redirecting outside Teams must include descriptive text. Hyperlinks showing a hand cursor on hover are eligible for review: If label has external indicator → contributes to PASS. If no indicator or bare link → WARNING.
Raw standalone links and links without descriptive indicators must be flagged as WARNING. When the issue is observed in only one instance, the WARNING must indicate that other instances were not found.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
PASS – Buttons/hyperlinks clearly indicate external navigation (labels such as "Open in Browser", "View in Web Portal", ↗️ present). FAIL – Buttons redirect externally without descriptive labeling. WARNING – Bare/raw hyperlink with no context, or Hyperlink present with no external indicator but hand‑cursor appears on hover → Use: "There is a hyperlink added in bot response. Please verify the link." Scoped case (only one instance found): Use: "Warning captured for only one instance. Other instances (e.g., links in Help response) were not found."
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The 'More Info' button redirects externally with no indication. Suggested fix: rename to 'Open in Browser' or 'View in Web Portal'."
Warning Examples:
"Bot returned only a raw hyperlink without any context." "There is a hyperlink added in bot response. Please verify the link." "Warning captured for only one instance. Other instances (e.g., links in Help response) were not found."
Hyperlink Detection Log (List All Steps Where Found):
Step 1 (Welcome): [quote/describe the hyperlink label/placement] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 2 (Help): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 3 (view Prompts command: ): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 4 (Generic): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 5 (Invalid): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No]
Evidence:
[Insert screenshots: Welcome message, Help response, prompts list & command responses, generic/invalid responses, and hover state with pointer hand visible]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.20
Test Case Name: Broken or unresponsive links present in bot response

Objective:
To verify that every hyperlink and action button within the bot's responses (Adaptive Cards or Markdown) is fully functional and uses descriptive language to signal external navigation. Hyperlinks included in bot responses are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over the hyperlink text, it indicates external redirection and is considered a PASS scenario (when indicators/labels are present).

Detailed Execution Steps (Follow in Order)

Welcome Message Content
Trigger or reset the bot to display the Welcome message.
Capture all links/buttons present in the Welcome message (record label + target URL).
Check for external indicators in labels (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).
Help Command
Send help and capture the response.
Note any links/buttons present, plus any supported commands/actionables referenced.
Run Actionables from Help (If Present)
Execute all actionable items/supported commands listed in the help response (buttons/CTAs/explicit command names) and analyze their responses.
(Do not re-run help itself if it appears.)

View Prompts (Compose Tray)
Click View prompts/View suggestions and run all commands listed.
Skip running Help here if listed.
Analyze these command responses for any links/buttons.

Generic Commands
Send "hi" and "hello"; capture responses.

Invalid Command
Send one invalid input (e.g., "xyz123"); capture the response.
Link Verification Scope (Apply to Steps 1 & 6)
For all links found in Step 1 (Welcome) and Step 6 (Invalid):
Connectivity: Click each link/button/image-map.
Pass: Opens correct destination page.
Fail: 404, timeout, broken, or placeholder (https://example.com).
Indicator Check: Verify label contains an external indicator (e.g., Open in Browser, View in…, ↗️).
Hover Behavior: Hover over text hyperlinks and confirm the cursor changes to a hand icon.
Note: Result classification (PASS/FAIL/WARNING) is driven by links present in Steps 1 and 6. Steps 2–5 are executed to surface additional UX, but link pass/fail/warning applies only to the Welcome and Invalid responses as per your instruction.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's 1:1 chat conversation, NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before capturing. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence, and its store tiles/buttons must NEVER be treated as the bot's links.
WELCOME links (Step 1 — observation FIRST, before typing anything): Anchor the view by scrolling the conversation to the TOP so the "added to chat" installation notification is visible; the Welcome message is the bot message immediately following it. WAIT until the page is fully settled and the Welcome card is completely rendered, then capture ONE settled screenshot showing the Welcome message with its links/buttons and labels. If the card is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so every link/button/label is evidenced. Capture ONLY the Welcome card — not the empty-state landing screen, the store page, or later command output.
HELP / generic / INVALID responses (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): For each required input, locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = the command (e.g., ["help"], then ["hi"], then ["hello"], then ["xyz123"]). Warning Example:

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.14
Test Case Name: Duplicate bot commands listed. Bots must not contain duplicate commands.

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot's command menu (defined in the app manifest) does not contain redundant or identical commands. Duplicate entries clutter the UI and confuse users.
Bot responses may also include hyperlinks in welcome or help messages; these are allowed. If the cursor changes to a hand icon on hover, it indicates external navigation and is a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Click View Prompts (Compose Tray):

Open View Prompts / View Suggestions from the compose tray.
Verify whether any listed command names or their descriptions match each other (identify same name duplicates and same/near‑identical descriptions implying duplicate functional intent).
Type Help & Check Actionables:

Send help and check if the response contains listed actionable / supported commands (buttons, CTAs, or clearly enumerated commands).
Verify whether any duplicate commands are present within the Help response (same names or different names with identical descriptions/intent).

Generate a WARNING if no View Prompts / no suggested commands are found in Step 1.

Verify Across Scopes:

Review both Personal and Channel scopes to confirm no duplicates exist within each respective context.

Screenshot Capture (evidence):
FIRST, make sure you are inside the bot's chat (1:1 for Personal scope, and the channel where the bot is added for Channel scope), NOT on the Teams Apps store / app listing / install page. If the current view is the Apps catalog or the app's install/details page, click Open to enter the bot chat before typing anything. The Apps store/listing page must NEVER be used as this test's evidence.
VIEW PROMPTS list (Step 1 — observation): Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray, wait for it to fully render and settle, and capture ONE settled screenshot of the full command list so EVERY command's name AND description is legible (this is the primary evidence for detecting same‑name or same‑description duplicates). If the list is taller than the viewport, scroll one section at a time and capture a SEPARATE settled screenshot per position so no command is cut off.
WARNING path: If no View Prompts / no suggested commands are found, capture ONE settled screenshot of the empty/absent compose-tray prompts as the WARNING evidence.
HELP response (interaction — use the deterministic capture tool): Locate the chat input field and call capture_input_responses with input_field_id = the chat field and inputs = ["help"]. The call types the input, submits it, waits for the bot's RESPONSE to fully render and settle, and captures the settled response. The evidence frame must show the bot's settled list of actionables/supported commands, NOT the moment of typing. Scroll-per-section with a SEPARATE settled screenshot if the help response is taller than the viewport so every listed command/CTA is legible for duplicate comparison.
SCOPE coverage: Repeat the View Prompts capture (and the help capture where applicable) in BOTH Personal and Channel scopes, so duplicate-within-scope evidence exists for each context. Label which scope each frame belongs to.
Capture-only guard: Every frame must show the bot's own command menu or help response. Do NOT substitute the Apps store page, the empty-state landing screen, or unrelated chat content.
These captures must be the LAST actions before reporting the verdict. Do NOT report PASS/FAIL/WARNING until the View Prompts list (per scope) and the help response have been captured and settled.
On FAIL, ensure the settled screenshot clearly shows the duplicate commands — same name appearing twice, or two differently named commands with identical descriptions/intent — as failure evidence. On WARNING, ensure the screenshot shows that no supported commands are present in View Prompts.
Ensure every captured frame reflects the final rendered state, not a transitional/loading frame.
Expected Result:

Each command must be unique in both name and purpose.
Hyperlinks in bot messages are acceptable when the cursor switches to a hand icon.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING
Result Conditions:

✅ PASS

All commands and their descriptions in View Prompts are unique.
Any commands listed in the Help response are also unique.

❌ FAIL

View Prompts contains commands with the same name or same description; or
Help response lists duplicate commands (same name or different names with identical descriptions/intent).

⚠️ WARNING

No View Prompts / no suggested commands are found.
Use this WARNING message:
"Warning (No supported commands found in View Prompts; kindly review bot behaviour)."

Failure Reasons (If applicable):

FAIL Example: "The command 'View Tasks' appears twice in the menu. Suggested correction: remove the duplicate entry."
FAIL Example: "'Help' and 'Support' both display identical content. They should be distinct."

Warning Example:
"Warning (No supported commands found in View Prompts; kindly review bot behaviour)."

Evidence:
Attach screenshots of:

Command menu (View Prompts / View Suggestions)
Help response (showing listed actionables/supported commands)
Any repeated or missing commands
